title: MPs and gifts: what's ok?
content:
Dear friend,

Thanks to Nigel Farage and the gifts he received from a crypto billionaire, political donations are all over the news again.

It’s not the first such story, nor will it be the last — but whatever your feelings about this particular matter, we believe that standards must be raised around money in politics.

We’ve been looking more closely at the gifts MPs receive. Who do they come from, and is it always fine for our politicians to receive them? We’d love to hear your views.

  • Is it OK for MPs to get paid for making controversial posts on X?
  • is it OK for MPs to receive tickets to sought-after events?
  • should MPs receive any gifts or donations at all?

    • We want to hear what you think: head over to our survey and let us know.

    Better standards start with transparency.

    Through our WhoFundsThem project, we’re working to build better information about the sources of funding politicians receive. We've been exploring links between gifts given to MPs and the questions they ask in Parliament — and a complaint we made, based on what we found, has already resulted in a formal apology from one MP.
snippet:
Dear friend,

Thanks to Nigel Farage and the gifts he received from a crypto billionaire, political donations are all over the news again.

title: Together we can push for better access to information
content:
Dear friend,

Last week, Julia braved the heatwave and hopped on the Eurostar to the Netherlands, where she joined campaigners, journalists, lawyers and researchers who work around Freedom of Information, across Europe and beyond.

This was a coming together of the ATI Network Community of Practice — and also a chance to share expertise in advance of the Dutch government’s review of their Open Government Act (charmingly known as “Woo”).

As Julia remarks in her account of the day: “Beyond the work of our own organisations, international attention matters to those in power. Knowing that colleagues across Europe and beyond are following developments, celebrating progress and raising concerns when transparency comes under pressure can strengthen domestic advocacy.

“By continuing to learn from one another, we stand a much better chance of making the right to information work not just on paper, but in practice.”

Read the full blog post here.

Your donations support activity like this, where we can convene organisations who care about transparency, and empower us all by sharing knowledge, experience and skills.

title: Burnham wants to reform the whip
content:
Dear friend,

In a speech introducing his political agenda, the (in-all-likelihood) future Prime Minister Andy Burnham discussed changes to the whipping system in Westminster.

This opens many questions about whipping. Should MPs be free to choose how they vote? If so, how do you ensure the government keeps the promises it made on election?

And are there ways to involve all MPs in deciding the party line, all the better to represent their constituencies?

If these questions interest you, dig into our full analysis in this blog post — or if you're in a hurry, Julia has summarised key points in a short video here.

You’re receiving this email because you indicated that you’re interested in democracy & parliaments; or mySociety’s activities in general. 

Not quite right? Adjust your preferences here.
snippet:
Dear friend,

In a speech introducing his political agenda, the (in-all-likelihood) future Prime Minister Andy Burnham discussed changes to the whipping system in Westminster.

title: MPs and gifts: the results are in
content:
Dear friend,

Last week, we asked you to share your feelings about MPs receiving gifts — and you certainly told us what you think.

We promised we’d share the results, so here they are:

  • 73.9% say that MPs shouldn’t receive any gifts at all
  • 18.4% think that some types of gift should not be allowed
  • 7.7% told us it’s ok for MPs to receive gifts, so long as they declare them.

    • With gifts and donations to MPs dominating the news headlines, and a new call from the Ethics and Integrity commission for all lobbying to be declared, your responses certainly reflect the national mood.

    92.3% of you believe there should be a change. And we suspect that even those of you who fall within the remaining 7.7% would like to know that the system for MPs declaring gifts is consistently applied.

    That's one of the things we’re pushing for through our WhoFundsThem project.

    “MPs often receive free tickets to expensive and sought-after events such as the Chelsea Flower Show, big pop concerts or high-profile football matches. These come from donors including banks, car manufacturers and the whisky industry.

    “Is this a perk of the job for hardworking MPs, or a step too far?”
snippet:
Dear friend,

Last week, we asked you to share your feelings about MPs receiving gifts — and you certainly told us what you think.

We promised we’d share the results, so here they are:

title: We want better standards in politics
content:
Dear friend,

mySociety’s WhoFundsThem project is working to build better information about money in politics, and raise standards of transparency and behaviour.

As well as our work on TheyWorkForYou to make the registers of interest more accessible, we’ve been busy behind the scenes, exploring how we can more directly get MPs to follow the rules, and raise the standard of their disclosures.

As part of this, we discovered a set of written questions by an MP, which should have included a declaration of interest. We submitted a complaint to the Parliamentary Commissioner. The complaint was upheld and the MP has now apologised.

You can read our in-depth explanation on what we submitted, and why we think it was important, in this blog post by Alex.

We’ve been experimenting with automated ways of scanning the register of gifts that MPs declare, and comparing this with the questions they ask in Parliament — helping us detect when an MP’s public role is potentially overlapping with private interests. 

We’ve set up code to screen the thousands of questions that MPs submit each week in Parliament.

We’ve already found many matches that merit further investigation.
snippet:
Dear friend,

mySociety’s WhoFundsThem project is working to build better information about money in politics, and raise standards of transparency and behaviour.

title: The data's in!
content:
Dear friend,

We promised we'd let you know when we'd added councillors’ email addresses to WriteToThem.com after the recent elections. Well...

Everything’s ready for you to contact your local representatives, whichever part of the UK you live in.

We're not saying everyone should send an email to their politicians right now. But if you have a tricky problem and you've tried other avenues, sometimes getting a councillor or even an MP involved can make all the difference.

Equally, if you think something's wrong, in your local area or nationally, writing to politicians is one route to getting things changed.

Of course, you might have nothing to complain about at the moment — lucky you! Keep WriteToThem in your bookmarks… and don’t forget to spread the word to friends and family who might need it.

WriteToThem makes it very easy to send an email to your politicians. You don't even have to know who they are — just type your postcode on the homepage and you'll see them all listed out.
snippet:
Dear friend,

We promised we'd let you know when we'd added councillors’ email addresses to WriteToThem.com after the recent elections. Well...

title: Have there been big changes in your area?
content:
Dear friend,

Last month, millions of people across the UK went out and exercised one of their democratic rights: voting for local and national representatives. Were you one of them?

In England, 5,066 councillors were elected across 136 of the 317 English authorities (local elections are stepped, so not everyone votes at the same time).

In Wales and Scotland, changes were national. The Senedd grew from 60 to 96 Members, with six representing each of the 16 reconfigured constituencies; and in Scotland, the people were asked to select all 129 MSPs.

Many representatives returned; others are taking up positions for the first time. All in all, there’s been a lot of change.

So… what now?

Whether you voted or not, your representatives are there for YOU... and mySociety’s services offer a number of different ways to engage with them.

We publish debates and votes from the UK Parliament and each of the devolved parliaments on TheyWorkForYou. Enter your postcode to see your MP’s or devolved Members’ pages, rich with information about how they’ve voted and what they've said.
snippet:
Dear friend,

Last month, millions of people across the UK went out and exercised one of their democratic rights: voting for local and national representatives. Were you one of them?

title: Reminder: Building an effective EU Civic Tech Hub
content:
Dear friend,

Just a reminder to join us THIS FRIDAY as we present our research around the European Commission’s proposed European Civic Tech Hub, an initiative intended to increase democratic participation and resilience across the region.

And please note, the date has changed since our initial mail-out: we'll be online on Friday 5 June at 12 noon, UK time.

Join us at our online report launch event to hear and discuss our key findings, drawing on insights from 19 interviews with civic tech practitioners from across Europe.

All attendees will receive a copy of the report.

This latest TICTeC work to improve the global impacts of civic technology is in collaboration with Sitra, the Finnish Innovation Fund.

Hope to see you there!

You’re receiving this email because you indicated that you’re interested in one of these topics: civic tech & research; mySociety conferences & events; or mySociety’s activities in general - or because you have been a TICTeC attendee who opted into receiving our communications.

Not quite right? Adjust your preferences here.

title: Report launch - Supporting participation: Building an effective EU Civic Tech Hub
content:
Dear friend,

mySociety’s latest TICTeC work to improve the global impacts of civic technology has taken its form in a collaboration with Sitra, the Finnish Innovation Fund.

​Together, we have been conducting research exploring the development of a proposed European Civic Tech Hub, which the European Commission has committed to creating as part of its Democracy Shield.

​Join us at our online report launch event to hear and discuss key findings from the research, drawing on insights from 19 interviews with civic tech practitioners from across Europe.

​The report explores what the EU Civic Tech Hub could do to strengthen the demand for, supply of, and implementation of civic tech across Europe, helping it to scale so more citizens can engage in democratic processes.

​There will be time for audience questions and discussion, and the session will be recorded. All attendees will receive a copy of the report.

​Speakers:

  • ​Julia Cushion, Policy and Advocacy Manager, mySociety
  • ​Kirsi Hantula, Head of Sitra’s Democracy Technology team
  • ​Gemma Moulder, Events and Engagement Manager, mySociety
  • ​Alex Parsons, Democracy Lead and Senior Researcher, mySociety

    • Hope to see you there!

title: Sewage in your local river?
content:
It’s something most people agree on, whatever their political persuasion: “Just at a fundamental level, it feels wrong for sewage to be going into rivers”. That's what Alex Lipp, one of the two people behind sewagemap.co.uk, told us when we chatted recently.

Feel the same? Thanks to his website, and the way it links up with our WriteToThem service, you can do something about it. First, see where the discharges are happening in your area. Then second, use the link Alex has incorporated into the site to hop across to WriteToThem and let your MP know what you think.

There's a strong argument here for historic data on sewage spills, as well, so that we can all understand whether the situation is improving or worsening; and to give campaigns for clean waterways the information they need to make their point.

To get the whole picture, and hear from Alex about why he made the SewageMap website — and the effects it's already had — head over to our blog post.

You’re receiving this email because you indicated that you’re interested in one of these topics: democracy & parliaments; how to make change; tools for campaigning; climate action; or mySociety’s activities in general.
